identity.title: Motor Vehicle Gasoline Engine and Engine Parts Manufacturing

lens.included_activities: Independent LMM manufacturers of gasoline engines and engine parts serving repeat external OEM, aftermarket, remanufacturing, and distributor customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive OEM plants, distributors without manufacturing, repair shops, shell brands, and financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat per-unit or per-lot sales under OEM supply programs, aftermarket replenishment, and standing purchase orders.

a.bridge: BLS measures broader motor-vehicle-parts employment: miscellaneous assemblers are 23.77%, metal workers 18.41%, and engine assemblers 4.47%. I assign AI exposure mainly to planning, quoting, procurement, documentation, engineering support, and customer service, not assembly, machining, testing, maintenance, or handling. The four-digit mix may misstate engine-specific machining and engineering.
a.rationale: Most labor is embodied production; information workflows offer a smaller exposed share.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task study at 15-25 independent engine-parts plants would materially change this estimate.
caveats: No six-digit task mix was found.
The frozen labor ratio mixes 2024 wages and 2022 receipts.

rho.rationale: ERP, CAD, quality, and commercial copilots are implementable, but OEM validation, legacy drawings, traceability, cybersecurity, and human approvals constrain realization.
rho.change_evidence: Plant before-and-after labor, quality, quote-cycle, and schedule data from AI deployments would change the range.
caveats: No representative LMM deployment panel exists.

e.rationale: Independent suppliers usually have repeat external OEM or aftermarket customers; captive plants, nonmanufacturing brands, and out-of-band firms are ineligible.
e.change_evidence: A firm census linking ownership, recurring external revenue, domestic production, and normalized EBITDA would change eligibility.
caveats: The assigned 37-firm count is margin-bridged, not observed.

s5.rationale: Tooling, qualifications, customer programs, and aftermarket positions are transferable, but no eligible-firm transfer denominator was found.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, GF Data, Capital IQ, UCC ownership changes, and auto-supplier broker interviews would change the range.
caveats: Product-line and plant asset sales are not necessarily control transfers.

q.rationale: Per-part pricing permits temporary retention, but OEM annual cost-downs, open-book purchasing, warranty terms, rebids, and aftermarket competition return substantial benefit to customers.
q.change_evidence: OEM and distributor contracts showing cost-downs, indexation, rebates, and productivity sharing would change retention.
caveats: Contract terms vary sharply between OEM and branded aftermarket products.

d5.bridge: EIA projected EVs at 15% of 2030 light-duty sales in its reference case, while DOE states all-electric vehicles have far fewer moving parts and no gasoline engine. I translate accelerating powertrain substitution into declining five-year demand for the current gasoline-engine basket; this may overstate decline because hybrids retain engines and the installed fleet sustains aftermarket demand, or understate it if policy and adoption accelerate.
d5.rationale: ICE fleet replacement and hybrids cushion demand, but new-vehicle electrification structurally removes gasoline engines and many related parts.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year OEM powertrain build schedules and aftermarket engine-part volumes by vehicle age would materially change the range.
caveats: EV-sales projections are not direct six-digit shipment forecasts.

o.rationale: Remaining gasoline-engine demand requires physical machining, assembly, testing, and accountable supply. Software cannot manufacture the parts, though EV substitution eliminates entire units of demand.
o.change_evidence: OEM insourcing, additive manufacturing, remanufacturing, and engine-elimination roadmaps would change this estimate.
caveats: Volume elimination belongs in d5; o applies only to remaining demand.

narrative.executive_view: A physically durable but structurally declining ICE supplier niche: AI mainly improves overhead workflows, while electrification reduces the current product basket.
narrative.ai_impact: AI assists quoting, planning, procurement, engineering search, quality records, and customer support; production remains embodied.
narrative.value_capture: OEM procurement practices reclaim much of the benefit, with better retention in differentiated aftermarket products.
narrative.firm_availability: Most independents fit repeat supply, but only 37 estimated target firms exist and transfer data are unobserved.
narrative.demand_durability: The installed ICE fleet and hybrids sustain near-term demand, while battery-electric adoption removes engines and many parts.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Electrification speed, OEM concentration, warranty exposure, tariffs, tooling obsolescence, and policy shifts dominate uncertainty.
narrative.principal_driver: Large installed ICE fleet supporting repeat OEM and replacement demand.
narrative.principal_weakness: Structural powertrain substitution and low information-work share.

sources.title: BLS May 2023 OEWS: Motor Vehicle Parts Manufacturing
sources.supports: Reports 557,020 jobs, including 23.77% miscellaneous assemblers, 18.41% metal workers, and 4.47% engine and machine assemblers.

sources.title: EIA Annual Energy Outlook: IRA Issues in Focus
sources.supports: Projects EVs at 15% of 2030 light-duty sales in the reference case, with 12% and nearly 17% in alternative cases.

sources.title: DOE Alternative Fuels Data Center: EV Maintenance and Safety
sources.supports: States all-electric vehicles have fewer moving parts and fluids and require less maintenance, while hybrids retain internal-combustion engines.

sources.title: EPA Greenhouse Gas Regulations for Passenger Cars and Trucks
sources.supports: Describes standards phasing in over model years 2027-2032 that leverage advances in clean vehicle technology.
